data.title: Wall Street Circles Starbucks as China Deal Heats Up--Tech Giants and PE Titans in Bidding War
data.description:
Starbucks is quietly making moves in Chinaand Wall Street's watching. The coffee giant has invited a dozen heavyweight investors, including Carlyle, KKR , EQT, and tech majors JD.com and Tencent (TCEHY), into the next phase of bidding for a stake in its China operations. According to people familiar with the matter, these shortlisted parties are now reviewing financials and preparing formal proposals. Starbucks CEO Brian Niccol emphasized this isn't about raising capital. What this is about, he told analysts, is how do we ensure that the Starbucks brand is in a much better place in the future. Starbucks plans to retain a meaningful stake and is not looking to fully exit.

The stakes are high. China is Starbucks' second-biggest marketbut it's also one of the most competitive. Local rival Luckin Coffee has surged ahead with cheaper drinks, faster innovation, and aggressive expansion. To stay relevant, Starbucks has started adapting its menu with more affordable fruit teas and sugar-free options. And early signs suggest it's working: same-store sales in China turned positive last quarter for the first time since late 2023. A local partner with tech and consumer insight could help Starbucks move faster, optimize supply chains, and deepen its mobile platform strategy in a market that increasingly rewards speed and price.

This process has drawn interest from over 20 potential backers, though only a dozen have made it to this second round. Starbucks isn't seeking a full salebut it is exploring how to tap deeper into China's fast-evolving consumer landscape. While no deal is guaranteed, more players could enter later stages as discussions evolve. With long-term ambitions to grow its China store count from roughly 7,800 to 20,000, the right strategic partner could be a pivotal lever for Starbucks' next act in Asia.

data.title: Caribbean activists celebrate as Saint Lucia strikes down gay sex ban
data.description:
Key points:
  • Activists say ruling is a start to gradual social change
  • More than 60 countries worldwide criminalize gay sex
  • Many Caribbean nations retain British colonial-era bans

Caribbean gay rights activists celebrated a landmark court ruling this week striking down colonial-era legislation in the island nation of Saint Lucia that criminalized gay sexual relations and imposed prison terms of up to a decade.

The Eastern Caribbean Supreme Court issued the decision on Tuesday in the case brought by LGBTQ rights alliance ECADE on behalf of a gay couple.

The court ruled that sections of the criminal code that outlawed "buggery" and "gross indecency" were unconstitutional.

"Our own courts are now recognizing that these colonial-era laws are incompatible with human dignity," Dane Lewis, regional program manager at rights group CariFLAGS, said.

Many Caribbean countries still have laws forbidding intimacy between people of the same sex, a legacy of British colonial-era statutes. Though rarely enforced, activists say these cement widespread institutional biases and discrimination.

Jessica St. Rose, founder of local rights group 758Pride, said the ruling marked a "momentous legal change."

"It sends a clear message that love is not a crime," she said, though Saint Lucia still needs reforms to protect LGBTQ people from discrimination and threats to their safety.

Saint Lucian politicians were mostly silent about the decision publicly, including Prime Minister Philip Pierre who made a national address ahead of Friday's Emancipation Day.

In nearby Guyana, where "buggery" is a felony subject to a lifetime behind bars, rights group Guyana Together welcomed that another country in the CARICOM regional bloc had "dismantled these archaic laws."

More than 60 countries worldwide criminalize gay sex, many former British colonies across Africa and the Caribbean.

There was some criticism on social media from Saint Lucia residents of the decision, some citing Christian scripture and calling the ruling a sin.

"We do expect the religious society to come out to speak out against the recent ruling," St. Rose said.

Bradley Desir, a gay man from Saint Lucia who moved to Canada in 2016, said he was encouraged by signs of change and would feel safer visiting the island though he would still maintain his guard.

"I hope they carry on with the discussion and possibly call for the legalization of (gay) marriage," he said, adding he did not expect this in his lifetime.

The growing visibility of LGBTQ people through global media was a positive sign, he added: "Kids today are growing up in a different world."

data.title: BYD Just Blinked -- China's EV Throne Now Up for Grabs
data.description:
BYD Co. (BYDDF), the world's largest EV seller, just hit a speed bump. July deliveries came in at 344,296 unitsbasically flat from a year ago and down 10% month-on-monthmarking the company's first monthly decline of 2025. While summer slowdowns aren't new in China's auto market, the timing couldn't be worse. With only 2.49 million vehicles sold year-to-date, BYD now needs to average 602,000 units per month to hit its 5.5 million goal. That's a steep climb, especially considering its all-time monthly high is still under 515,000. To make matters tougher, Beijing is cracking down on discount-driven price wars, tightening the screws on the entire sector.

Meanwhile, the competition is heating up fast. Geely just posted its strongest month since last November with 237,717 units. Xpeng and Leapmotor both notched record-breaking deliveries36,717 and 50,129 vehicles, respectivelywhile Xiaomi broke through the 30,000-unit mark for the first time. Even Nio, despite logging its weakest month since March, saw its stock jump 8.6% after the launch of its aggressively priced Onvo L90, which some analysts view as a strong product-market fit. Li Auto, on the other hand, took a hitdown 40% year-on-year to just over 30,000 units.

Zooming out, retail passenger vehicle sales in China were estimated to grow 7.6% year-over-year in July but fell 11% versus June, reflecting broader seasonal softness. Still, the narrative around BYD could be shifting. It's not just about volume anymoreit's about defending market share in a field that's suddenly full of fast-moving challengers. With delivery targets slipping out of reach and new rivals scaling up at speed, the race for EV dominance in China is looking a lot more competitive.

data.title: OpenAI raises $8.3B at $300B valuation
data.description:
OpenAI has raised $8.3 billion at a $300 billion valuation, accelerating its plan to secure $40 billion in funding by year’s end. The latest round, led by Dragoneer Investment Group, comes after the company raised $2.5 billion in March.

According to The New York Times on Friday, Dragoneer committed $2.8 billion to the raise, representing roughly 10% of its total funds. The funding round brings OpenAI closer to its 2025 target, which includes a $30 billion commitment from SoftBank.

The funding round was five times oversubscribed, meaning investors wanted to invest roughly $40 billion, the Times said. OpenAI prioritized new strategic investors over existing backers, frustrating some investors who received smaller allocations than they hoped for.

Other investors include Blackstone, TPG, Sequoia Capital, Fidelity Management, Andreessen Horowitz, Altimeter Capital, Coatue Management, D1 Capital Partners, Thrive Capital and Tiger Global.

A brief history of OpenAI

OpenAI was founded in 2015 by Elon Musk, Sam Altman, Greg Brockman, Ilya Sutskever and others as a nonprofit research lab committed to ensuring artificial general intelligence benefits humanity.

In 2019, it created a capped-profit subsidiary, OpenAI LP, to raise outside funding, securing a $1 billion investment from Microsoft and transitioning toward a more commercially driven model. 

OpenAI expects to generate $12.7 billion in total revenue in 2025, according to internal projections reported by Bloomberg in March. 

More recently, DealBook reported that the company’s annual recurring revenue has reached $13 billion and is projected to surpass $20 billion by year-end. In September, OpenAI reported reaching 1 million paid users for its business-focused ChatGPT plans.

ChatGPT has also dominated its Large Language Model competitors. Data from FirstPageSage shows it has steadily maintained more than 70% of the LLM market share since January 2024.

Despite surging adoption, OpenAI doesn’t expect to be cash-flow positive until 2029, when revenue could top $125 billion. 

AI-focused crypto projects attract fresh venture capital

While OpenAI continues to attract record-breaking investment from traditional venture firms, the crypto industry is carving out its own AI frontier. A growing wave of decentralized AI startups is raising capital to build open-source, token-powered alternatives to proprietary models.

In April, AI startup Nous Research raised $50 million in a Series A round led by Paradigm, valuing the company at $1 billion. Nous is developing open-source AI models on the Solana blockchain to provide decentralized alternatives to platforms like OpenAI and DeepSeek.

In July, Poseidon raised $15 million in seed funding led by a16z Crypto. The US-based full-stack AI data layer aims to solve the shortage of high-quality, legally cleared training data for AI models by providing structured, real-world data sets that can be used without copyright concerns.

Overall, crypto venture funding has surged in Q2 of 2025 to over $10 billion, its best quarter since early 2022. In June alone, $5.14 billion was raised.

NVO: Novo Nordisk Stock Climbs After Major FDA Approval
data.description:
Aug 1 - Novo Nordisk gained nearly 3% in Friday morning trading after the company secured expanded U.S. approval for its Alhemo injection. The therapy can now help prevent or reduce bleeding episodes in adults and children 12 and older with hemophilia A or B.

Previously, Alhemo was only authorized for patients with hemophilia A or B who developed inhibitors. The new decision from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration followed a late?stage clinical trial that demonstrated the drug's safety and ability to lower bleeding risk in a broader patient group.

Analysts say this approval could significantly expand Alhemo's market potential. The hemophilia space is becoming increasingly competitive with gene therapies and new clotting factor treatments, but Novo Nordisk's broader label should reinforce its rare disease portfolio.

The company expects Alhemo to contribute to revenue growth in the second half of 2025 as more patients gain access. Investors will watch whether this momentum continues into upcoming quarters as the therapy reaches a larger base of hemophilia patients.

data.title: U.S. Natural Gas Futures Hold Above Key Support — Market Talk
data.description:
1540 ET - U.S. natural gas futures settle a couple of cents lower in range-bound trade, holding above the key $3 level with support from some warmer weather seen later this month and a pickup in LNG feedgas flows. "The bearishness seemed to hit exhaustion levels and the traders that have a target of $3 to take profit apparently did so," Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group says in a note. "It sure helped that we got a little bit of heat as well." Nymex natural gas settles down 0.7% at $3.083/mmBtu, for a 2.4% weekly loss. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1539 ET - Mexico received $5.2 billion in remittances in June, down 16% from the year-earlier month in a third consecutive decline, Bank of Mexico data show. Remittances in the first half of the year were down 5.6%, at $29.6 billion. "The flow of remittances to Mexico has experienced a streak of 11 consecutive years of growth, which will most likely end in 2025," BBVA's Juan Jose Li Ng says in a note. A high base of comparison is one of the main factors explaining the double-digit declines in April and June of this year, he says. "Recent U.S. government immigration actions and policies are only marginally affecting the decline in remittances to Mexico, which have shown signs of uncertainty over the past 20 months, a trend that began to be observed in November 2023." (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1519 ET - Most-active live cattle futures and lean hog futures on the CME finished the day on the positive side. Live cattle closed up 0.2% to $2.246 a pound, and lean hog futures closed up 0.6% to 90.175 cents a pound. For both, it extends a winning trend that started in late July, continuing to the first day of August. With today's close, both cattle and hog futures have closed higher for four of the past five trading sessions, according to FactSet data. It was a notable month for cattle, setting record-highs multiple times, including a new all-time high of $2.30 a pound hit Wednesday. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1136 ET - LBBW expects that credit spreads on euro corporate bonds will widen by less than it previously forecast. The eurozone's economic outlook is improving after governments pledged to raise spending on defense and infrastructure, LBBW's Michael Koehler says in a note. "Planned government investment programs--particularly in Germany--are providing a cautious boost to sentiment." Very-tight credit spreads are likely to widen moderately, he says. BBB-rated euro credit spreads are forecast to widen to around 110 basis points by September, below LBBW's earlier forecast of 135 basis points. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1129 ET - CBOT soybean futures are up 0.3% in morning trading, slightly reversing the losing streak seen in soybeans across the past five trading sessions, and eight out of the past nine trading days. Soybeans fell under the $10/bushel mark and shed nearly 10% of their value in that time. Fund traders have taken an increasingly-negative view on the prospects for futures, Matt Zeller of StoneX says in a note. "Soybeans have taken the brunt of the funds' wrath heading into the key month of August, starting to build up a moderate overall net short according to daily fund estimates." The CFTC will update its outlook for the Commitment of Traders report this afternoon, with short positions in soybeans built. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1121 ET - Amazon didn't explicitly say AWS could accelerate in 3Q but analysts at BofA Securities think it can. The internet retailer's cloud-computing arm reported sales climbed 17.5%, which is weighing on its stock given lofty investor expectations. The analysts say that Amazon is still capacity constrained and growing capex. They also point out that AWS added the second-most revenue dollars quarter-over-quarter in its history despite these constraints. That occurred as its backlog grew $6 billion quarter-over-quarter, which is below peers, but year-over-year growth accelerated to 25%. "AWS lost ground to Azure in 2Q, but Cloud growth can be lumpy," the analysts say. "Though AWS is not seeing the benefit of ChatGPT usage, broader enterprise GenAI adoption remains a growth catalyst." Shares decline 7.6% to $216.23. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1113 ET - Gold futures rise after U.S. economic data came in softer than expected. Futures are up 1.6% at $3,402.30 a troy ounce. Nonfarm Payroll data showed just 73,000 jobs were added in July, below analyst expectations of 100,000. Even more suprising, revisions to May and June's data revised jobs down by a huge 258,000, ING analysts say in a note. This highlights that the jobs market has lost momentum even earlier than thought and will intensify pressure from President Trump for the Federal Reserve to loosen monetary policy, ING says. With tariffs set to eat into household spending and corporate profits, the case for cutting interest rates has been significantly strengthened, ING says. Lower interest rates typically benefit non-interest bearing bullion. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1036 ET - The European Central Bank will likely next move to cut interest rates in December, given weaker growth in the second half and softer inflation after some resilience in current activity, Barclays economist Mariano Cena says in a note. By December, there should be clear signals on trade headwinds, reduced concerns about the impact of supply-chain disruptions on inflation, and increased comfort that fiscal plans for 2026 won't reignite inflation pressures, he says. That supports a final 25 basis-point "insurance" cut. Still, risks of a persistent inflation undershoot suggest a need for policy flexibility toward the end of the year, Cena notes. Barclays earlier this week removed its previous expectation of an ECB rate cut in September. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

1031 ET - Uncertainty surrounding U.S. tariffs remains, Societe Generale's Wei Yao says in a note. The U.S. announced new tariffs on Canada, Switzerland, Taiwan, and other trade partners, with most delayed until August 7. "It is tempting to think that a large part of the tariff uncertainty is behind us now, but we remain cautious," Yao says. The current trade agreements are vague on details, she says. "Much confusion and many questions linger over all the investment commitments." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1005 ET - U.S. crops managed to make it out of June and July mostly unscathed, with the U.S. Drought Monitor reporting shrinking drought coverage of farmland across the country this week. Of Corn Belt states, only North Dakota and Nebraska are seeing any large areas affected by dryness, while for the rest of the Corn Belt dryness its practically nonexistent--making a record-sized crops forecast by the USDA this year more likely. "A year ago heat in the second half of August and rapid net soil moisture loss stole from potential from final yields," says Daniel Flynn of Price Futures Group in a note. CBOT corn falls 0.5%, soybeans slide 0.2%, and wheat is down 0.9%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0952 ET - Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack says she remains comfortable with the central bank's choice to hold rates steady this week even after seeing weakening job creation in the July jobs report Friday morning. In a TV interview with Bloomberg, Hammack says the Fed is right to be attentive to inflation, too. "We have to look at, if we're missing, by how much and for how long do we expect those misses to last? And right now, we're missing by much more on the inflation side than we're missing on the employment side." Hammack isn't a Fed voter this year. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

0950 ET - European stocks fall deeper into the red after the latest U.S. jobs report showed hiring activity slowed in the world's largest economy in July. The Stoxx Europe 600 is down 1.9%, while the narrower Euro Stoxx 50 of eurozone blue-chip stocks is down 2.9%. Germany's DAX falls 2.7%, France's CAC 40 sheds 3% and Italy's FTSE MIB is down 2.6%. Major European stock indexes have declined since the market opened after President Trump's tariff blitz, but losses widen after the jobs report. The U.S. added a seasonally adjusted 73,000 jobs in July, the Labor Department reported, below the gain of 100,000 jobs economists polled by The Wall Street Journal had forecast. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

data.title: Mexico's Remittances Fell Sharply in June — Market Talk
data.description: Mexico received $5.2 billion in remittances in June, down 16% from the year-earlier month in a third consecutive decline, Bank of Mexico data show. Remittances in the first half of the year were down 5.6%, at $29.6 billion. "The flow of remittances to Mexico has experienced a streak of 11 consecutive years of growth, which will most likely end in 2025," BBVA's Juan Jose Li Ng says in a note. A high base of comparison is one of the main factors explaining the double-digit declines in April and June of this year, he says. "Recent U.S. government immigration actions and policies are only marginally affecting the decline in remittances to Mexico, which have shown signs of uncertainty over the past 20 months, a trend that began to be observed in November 2023." (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1540 ET - U.S. natural gas futures settle a couple of cents lower in range-bound trade, holding above the key $3 level with support from some warmer weather seen later this month and a pickup in LNG feedgas flows. "The bearishness seemed to hit exhaustion levels and the traders that have a target of $3 to take profit apparently did so," Phil Flynn of the Price Futures Group says in a note. "It sure helped that we got a little bit of heat as well." Nymex natural gas settles down 0.7% at $3.083/mmBtu, for a 2.4% weekly loss. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1513 ET - Oil futures fall for a second straight session as a weak U.S. employment report adds economic worries to concerns about U.S. tariffs curbing demand. OPEC+ is expected to agree at the weekend to another large output increase for September, fully unwinding the 2.2 million barrels a day of November 2023 voluntary production cuts and covering the UAE's higher quota. Crude futures still end the week higher, having jumped earlier on U.S. tariff threats against buyers of Russian oil and optimism over U.S. trade deals with major partners including the EU, Japan and South Korea. WTI settles down 2.8%, at $67.33 a barrel, for a weekly gain of 3.3%. Brent falls 2.8%, to $69.67, up 3% on the week. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1338 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. fell by five this week to 410, or 72 fewer than a year ago, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. The 14th straight weekly drop leaves oil rigs at their lowest level since September 2021, as drilling efficiency, capital discipline and relatively low oil prices keep operators from ramping up activity. Rigs directed at natural gas increased for a third straight week, rising by two to 124 or 26 more than a year ago. "With the rig increases, we now forecast gas supply/demand balances narrowing to the 5-year average in 2H25, but remaining slightly oversupplied through 1Q26," analysts at UBS said in a report. "Our new timetable for balances to go undersupplied is now pushed out to mid-2026." (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1235 ET - Crude futures extend losses after U.S. July payrolls come in below expectations and job gains for the previous two months are revised down sharply. That adds to demand concerns with U.S. import tariffs set to take effect Aug. 7. "This is definitely a macro-driven selloff," says CIBC Private Wealth US senior energy trader Rebecca Babin. The tariff headlines "are shaping up to be a little bit on the worse side of what was expected." On the positive side, the soft employment data puts September in play for a Fed rate cut, "but weakening economic numbers is something that the market has really been fearing," Babin adds. Also on the bearish side, OPEC+ is expected to agree to another large output increase in September at its Sunday meeting. WTI is down 2.8% at $67.33 a barrel and Brent is off 2.9% at $69.66. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1053 ET - Overall, Imperial Oil had a good quarter, reckons Raymond James's Michael Barth. The analyst says the energy company managed a modest 4% beat on funds from operations over consensus estimates on what largely appears to be slightly stronger production. Still, given that Imperial is one of the best performing large-cap Canadian energy names, Barth is skeptical the latest quarter will be enough to drive meaningful outperformance against its peer group from here. Shares are down 1% at C$114.34. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0935 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are slightly lower after yesterday's recovery from a dip below $3. Prices could find support from August weather forecasts showing more heat for the 6-10 and 11-15 day periods, Tradition Energy's Gary Cunningham says in a note. Possible record temperatures next week in the southwest "could push electricity demands in southern California to levels requiring extra dispatch of gas peakers," he adds. "We expect the September contract to be range-bound between $3 and $3.20 with some resistance at $3.15." September gas is off 0.6% at $3.088/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0927 ET - Enbridge may have affirmed its Ebitda guidance for 2025 where investors may have wanted to see a lift given a strong 1H performance, but its results overall were solid, Jefferies' Sam Burwell says. The energy company's 2Q Ebitda beat the consensus forecast by 2.5%, driven by its U.S. gas transmission business, and it ended the quarter with debt leverage of 4.7 times, the analyst notes. Liquids earnings were in line with what Burwell anticipated, the renewables business was slightly below his forecast, but has transmission offered a solid beat and gas distribution was modestly ahead of his forecast. Jefferies has a one-year forward target of C$72 on the shares, which were last at C$62.75. (robb.stewart@wsj.com)

0912 ET - Oil futures flit between small gains and losses as the U.S. tariff deadline passes, with tariffs due to kick in on Aug. 7, fueling concerns about demand. "Values are being supported by the need to maintain risk premium for possibly another week on the chance that secondary tariffs may be placed on importers of Russian oil," Ritterbusch says in a note. "Oil price downside in response to expected weakening in economic and oil demand growth paths is being limited mainly to the product markets." WTI is up 0.1% at $69.30 a barrel, and Brent is down 0.1% at $71.65 as October moves to the front of the curve. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0724 ET - Oil prices fall, with Brent crude and WTI both down 1% at $71.01 a barrel and $68.55 a barrel respectively. Oil prices are higher on week but pressure could reassert itself as rising inventories and renewed trade tensions weigh on market sentiment, XTB MENA analysts say in a note. A surprise 7.7 million U.S. crude stock build has raised fresh concerns of slowing demand while cartel OPEC+ is set to increase production in September, which could weigh on prices, analysts say. President Trump's decision to impose import tariffs ranging from 10% to 41% on key trading partners has also reignited concerns of slower global growth and weaker fuel demand. The threat of secondary tariffs on Russian oil have raised the specter of disrupted trade flows, but broader economic protectionism implications could outweigh any positive effect, analysts add. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0402 ET - Oil prices are up significantly on week, with Brent and WTI 6% and 6.3% higher, respectively. With a better growth backdrop from positive economic data releases and concerns over tightening Russian supply in July, Brent crude posted its best month since September 2023, Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. Oil prices rallied late in the month after President Trump threatened new sanctions or secondary tariffs against importers of Russian oil, with a deadline of Aug. 8, analysts write. U.S. trade policy also appeared more successful over July, with a raft of trade deals signed late in the month, raising economic hopes and optimism for oil demand. In early morning trade, Brent crude is flat at $71.69 a barrel and WTI is down 0.1% at $69.22 a barrel. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

data.title: Fed Governor Adriana Kugler to Step Down — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Anita Hamilton

Federal Reserve Board Governor Adriana Kugler is stepping down from her job effective August 8.

"It has been an honor of a lifetime to serve on the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System," she said. "I am especially honored to have served during a critical time in achieving our dual mandate of bringing down prices and keeping a strong and resilient labor market."

No reason was given for her decision.

Kugler was nominated to the board by then-President Joe Biden and her term was set to end in January.

She wasn't present at the Fed's meeting Wednesday when it voted to hold rates steady, and she didn't vote.

Write to Anita Hamilton at anita.hamilton@barrons.com

data.title: OSHKOSH CORP SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Oshkosh Corp, a leading manufacturer of specialty vehicles and vehicle bodies, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial and operational performance, highlighting key metrics and business activities that have shaped the quarter.

Financial Highlights

Oshkosh Corp reported the following key financial metrics for the second quarter of 2025:

  • Net sales: $2,732.1 million, a decrease of 4.0% compared to the second quarter of 2024, primarily due to lower sales volume in the Access and Transport segments.
  • Gross income: $524.5 million, remained flat as improved sales mix and pricing were offset by higher production costs and warranty expenses.
  • Operating income: $291.7 million, an increase of 11.8% due to lower intangible asset impairments and improved sales mix.
  • Net income: $204.8 million, up from $168.6 million in the second quarter of 2024, reflecting improved operating income.
  • Diluted EPS: $3.16, an increase from $2.56 in the second quarter of 2024, benefiting from share repurchases and improved operating income.

Business Highlights

Oshkosh Corp's business performance for the second quarter of 2025 included the following highlights:

  • Revenue Segments: The Access segment, which includes aerial work platforms and telehandlers, reported a decrease in sales due to the expiration of the Caterpillar-branded telehandlers agreement and higher discounts. The Vocational segment saw increased sales volume, benefiting from higher pricing. The Transport segment's revenue reflected the ramp-up of production for the Next Generation Delivery Vehicles (NGDV) for the USPS.
  • Geographical Performance: Sales in the United States decreased by 4.6% in the second quarter of 2025 compared to the same period in 2024, while sales in Europe, Africa, and the Middle East increased by 22.6%, driven by higher demand in these regions. Sales in the Rest of the World decreased by 28.9%, reflecting weaker market conditions.
  • Sales Units: The Access segment experienced a decline in sales units due to the end of the Caterpillar-branded telehandlers agreement. The Vocational segment delivered higher sales units, particularly in municipal fire apparatus and airport products.
  • New Production Launches: The Transport segment continued the production ramp-up of the NGDV for the USPS, contributing to its revenue performance.
  • Future Outlook: The company expects its 2025 diluted earnings per share to be approximately $10.25, with adjusted earnings per share estimated at $11.00. The Access segment is projected to achieve sales of approximately $4.4 billion with an operating income margin of 11.5%. The Vocational segment is expected to reach sales of approximately $3.8 billion with a 15.0% operating income margin. The Transport segment is anticipated to achieve sales of approximately $2.3 billion with a 4.5% operating income margin.

SEC Filing: OSHKOSH CORP [ OSK ] - 10-Q - Aug. 01, 2025

data.title: Urals differentials stable, Kazakhstan's oil output dropped 2% in July
data.description:
Urals oil differentials to dated Brent remained stable on Friday, though concerns about India’s demand could put pressure on the grade’s values, traders said.

At least two vessels loaded with Russian oil bound for refiners in India have diverted to other destinations following new U.S. sanctions, trade sources said and LSEG trade flows showed.

It was not clear if the U.S. threat to impose penalties on India for its Russian oil imports would cause supply disruptions. As of Friday, most of supplies remained uninterrupted, traders said.

Kazakhstan's daily crude oil output, excluding gas condensate, a type of light oil, dropped to 1.84 million barrels per day in July from 1.88 million bpd in June, a source familiar with the data told Reuters.

PLATTS WINDOW

  • No bids or offers were made for Urals, Azeri BTC or CPC Blend in the Platts window on Friday.

NEWS

  • Russia could face gasoline shortages in August despite an export ban, as low domestic stocks, peak seasonal demand and repair work at domestic refineries affect the market, trade sources said on Friday.

  • Pakistan's largest refiner Cnergyico will import 1 million barrels of oil from Vitol in October, Cnergyico's Vice Chairman Usama Qureshi said on Friday, the country's first-ever purchase of U.S. crude following a landmark trade deal.

data.title: UK's online safety law is putting free speech at risk, X says
data.description:
Britain's online safety law risks suppressing free speech due to its heavy-handed enforcement, social media site X said on Friday, adding that significant changes were needed.

The Online Safety Act, which is being rolled out this year, sets tough new requirements on platforms such as Facebook, YouTube, TikTok and X, as well as sites hosting pornography, to protect children and remove illegal content.

But it has attracted criticism from politicians, free-speech campaigners and content creators, who have complained that the rules had been implemented too broadly, resulting in the censorship of legal content.

Users have complained about age checks that require personal data to be uploaded to access sites that show pornography, and more than 468,000 people have signed an online petition calling for the act to be repealed.

The government said on Monday it had no plans to do so and it was working with regulator Ofcom to implement the act as quickly as possible.

Technology Secretary Peter Kyle said on Tuesday that those who wanted to overturn it were "on the side of predators".

Elon Musk's X, which has implemented age verification, said the law's laudable intentions were at risk of being overshadowed by the breadth of its regulatory reach.

"When lawmakers approved these measures, they made a conscientious decision to increase censorship in the name of 'online safety'," it said in a statement.

"It is fair to ask if UK citizens were equally aware of the trade-off being made."

X said the timetable for meeting mandatory measures had been unnecessarily tight, and despite being in compliance, platforms still faced threats of enforcement and fines, encouraging over-censorship.

It said a balanced approach was the only way to protect liberty, encourage innovation and safeguard children.

"It's safe to say that significant changes must take place to achieve these objectives in the UK," it said.

A UK government spokesperson said it is "demonstrably false" that the Online Safety Act compromises free speech.

"As well as legal duties to keep children safe, the very same law places clear and unequivocal duties on platforms to protect freedom of expression," the spokesperson said.

Ofcom said on Thursday it had launched investigations into the compliance of four companies, which collectively run 34 pornography sites.
